episode_title: This Week #1: Must reads on growth, what PMs should focus on when joining a new team, and the value of an MBA
insight: By day 60, a new PM should shift to improving team execution, ensuring clarity, removing blockers, and streamlining processes to deliver tangible impact.
pull_quote: After 30 days, a new PM's focus shifts to improving execution. Ensure everyone knows what to do, meetings are efficient, and blockers are addressed quickly to drive tangible impact.

episode_title: This Week #1: Must reads on growth, what PMs should focus on when joining a new team, and the value of an MBA
insight: By day 90, a new PM must develop a strategic point of view, collaborate on a roadmap, and secure early stakeholder buy-in to avoid a failed plan.
pull_quote: By day 90, a new PM must develop their own strategic point of view. Work with the team to craft a 6-12 month roadmap, and get early buy-in from stakeholders to prevent your plan from flopping.

episode_title: This Week #1: Must reads on growth, what PMs should focus on when joining a new team, and the value of an MBA
insight: For an existing PM, an MBA is generally not worth it to accelerate your career; direct product experience consistently outweighs business school.
pull_quote: Thinking of an MBA to accelerate your PM career? Lenny says: In most cases, it’s not worth it. When hiring PMs, nothing beats direct experience doing the actual job over two years in business school.

episode_title: This Week #1: Must reads on growth, what PMs should focus on when joining a new team, and the value of an MBA
insight: To accelerate your PM career without an MBA, actively seek mentorship, diversify experience across products/teams, and continuously engage with PM communities.
pull_quote: Don't get an MBA to accelerate your PM career. Instead, talk to your manager, find a mentor, work on varied products/teams, switch companies for stronger teams, and consume industry resources like PMHQ.

episode_title: This Week #10: Keeping designers and engineers excited about metrics + Transitioning from DS to PM 🕺
insight: Involving the entire team in defining and reviewing metrics from the start is the best way to get them excited about growth.
pull_quote: Designers and engineers are most engaged when they are involved in goal setting and goal breakdown. When you define quarterly goals, invite your whole team to participate.

episode_title: This Week #10: Keeping designers and engineers excited about metrics + Transitioning from DS to PM 🕺
insight: Challenge every proposed project, including your own, by requiring teams to make a case with evidence for impact and clear success metrics.
pull_quote: Everyone should be able to explain why they want to prioritize something, what evidence they have for impact and how success should be evaluated. Occasionally, if someone really cares and it takes a couple of hours, let them do it.

episode_title: This Week #10: Keeping designers and engineers excited about metrics + Transitioning from DS to PM 🕺
insight: Internal transfer programs are the easiest, least risky, and most supported path to becoming a PM, despite potential leveling adjustments.
pull_quote: An internal transfer program is by far THE BEST way to do it. This path is the easiest, the least risky, and provides you with the most support.

episode_title: This Week #10: Keeping designers and engineers excited about metrics + Transitioning from DS to PM 🕺
insight: Data scientists becoming PMs must balance leveraging strong hard skills with proactively developing critical 'weird soft-skills' like influence and product taste.
pull_quote: Having a DS background is both a superpower and a liability. You’re strong at hard skills (e.g. impact, execution), but less experienced with weird soft-skills (e.g. influence, product taste).

episode_title: This Week #11: What should new PMs over-index on, and empowering product in a sales-driven org
insight: Early PMs should relentlessly prioritize shipping successful products; other tasks are distractions until your main project is a clear win.
pull_quote: Tackling lots of problems or taking on new responsibilities could be a distraction. As an early PM, you should first focus on doing everything you can to ensure your main project is a success and staying long enough to measure its impact.

episode_title: This Week #11: What should new PMs over-index on, and empowering product in a sales-driven org
insight: Being the first PM at a company, while exciting, can hinder learning if you lack strong mentorship; a product coach is essential.
pull_quote: If you've been promoted to be the first PM at your company, you won't learn as fast if you're trying to figure it all out yourself without a strong mentor. See if the company will pay for a product coach.

episode_title: This Week #11: What should new PMs over-index on, and empowering product in a sales-driven org
insight: Don't fall for the trap of ignoring resume-boosting companies; early career credentials provide a crucial safety net for future moves.
pull_quote: Be wary of those who say to ignore big tech companies; early career credentials from well-known places provide a crucial safety net. You only need enough to pass resume screening, but don't overlook this baseline early on.

episode_title: This Week #11: What should new PMs over-index on, and empowering product in a sales-driven org
insight: Your junior peers today will be senior leaders tomorrow; invest in building strong relationships early, both inside and outside your company.
pull_quote: The people you meet in your first few years might help you get a job later on, or be the people you recruit to your new startup. It's helpful to have senior people in your network, and the junior people you meet now might be senior by the time you need them.

episode_title: This Week #11: What should new PMs over-index on, and empowering product in a sales-driven org
insight: If product isn't driving growth and decision-making, you'll be a second-class citizen; move to a product-led company.
pull_quote: If your company is sales-led or marketing-led, product will always be a second-class citizen. You'll have the best time and learn the most at a product-led org, where product, eng, or design is driving the ship.

episode_title: This Week #12: Expanding your business internationally 🌏
insight: International expansion isn't just about new markets; it's a critical defense strategy to prevent foreign competitors from threatening your home turf.
pull_quote: Competitive dynamics can also be a reason to expand. If you aren’t careful, strong international competitors can threaten your home market — think HelloFresh vs. Blue Apron. Domestic competitors can use international expansion to raise more capital and become a threat.

episode_title: This Week #12: Expanding your business internationally 🌏
insight: Prioritize international markets by merging short-term potential (existing demand/supply) with long-term strategic value (TAM, execution ease).
pull_quote: Start by making two sorted lists of potential markets: short-term (existing demand/supply) and long-term (largest TAM, easiest to execute). Then, merge these into a single ranked list of top choices.

episode_title: This Week #12: Expanding your business internationally 🌏
insight: Achieve marketplace liquidity in new geos by starting small (e.g., one city), hitting a specific GMV target (e.g., $10k/30 days), then splitting or moving.
pull_quote: One of our favorite tactics is a divide and conquer cycle: Constrain geo, achieve liquidity, review geo (split or move). It’s helpful to set a clear definition of liquidity, like $10k in GMV over a rolling 30 day period.

episode_title: This Week #12: Expanding your business internationally 🌏
insight: When expanding internationally, prioritize nimble execution over rigid long-term processes, but relentlessly invest in documentation and data.
pull_quote: What works will change over time. Don’t over-invest in long-term processes (for now). Do invest heavily in documentation, data gathering, and knowledge sharing.

episode_title: This Week #13: Balancing outcome-thinking with design and technical requirements ⚖️
insight: Organize PM teams around achieving specific outcomes like "more bookings," not around owning product features, to drive significant impact.
pull_quote: Outcome-thinking was a key ingredient in Airbnb's success. Teams thought in outcomes to achieve, not features to build, and prioritized work around these goals. There was a strong correlation between significant impact and outcome-oriented teams.

episode_title: This Week #13: Balancing outcome-thinking with design and technical requirements ⚖️
insight: To balance short-term outcomes with critical long-term design/tech needs, extend your outcome thinking 1+ years out to evaluate future ROI.
pull_quote: To balance outcome-thinking with engineering and design needs (which often don’t directly drive that outcome), extend your outcome thinking further out into the future. How much would this work benefit your outcome 1+ years out?

episode_title: This Week #13: Balancing outcome-thinking with design and technical requirements ⚖️
insight: Evaluate major tech/design investments by laying out scenarios, assessing resources, short/long-term impact, and risks to determine ROI.
pull_quote: When deciding on major tech investments like migrating a monolith, lay out scenarios. Consider how long it takes, resources used, impact on immediate and long-term goals, and what risks it introduces or removes. It always comes back to ROI.

episode_title: This Week #14: Transitioning from startup founder to product manager
insight: Unlike founders focused on winning at all costs, PMs thrive by avoiding chaos, prioritizing teamwork, and mastering soft skills — hitting goals isn't enough.
pull_quote: As a founder your job is essentially to win at all costs. As a PM, your job is to *avoid* chaos, to *avoid* changing course, and particularly at a large company to *avoid* pissing people off.

episode_title: This Week #14: Transitioning from startup founder to product manager
insight: Acquired founders must retain their entrepreneurial drive; don't let large org inertia dull your ability to move fast and challenge the status quo.
pull_quote: Companies generally acquire other companies because they want to move faster on a given opportunity. Don’t let that discourage you. Take risks. Move fast. Push folks to think differently. This is why they brought you in.

episode_title: This Week #14: Transitioning from startup founder to product manager
insight: As a new PM, shed the founder ego; assume others are smarter, ask relentless questions, and deeply understand the business before sharing your perspective.
pull_quote: Startup founders often enter a company with a huge ego and an assumption that they know a lot more than everyone else. I suggest doing the opposite. Assume everyone else is smarter than you. Spend most of your time asking questions.

episode_title: This Week #14: Transitioning from startup founder to product manager
insight: As a PM, your boss's opinion directly impacts your career trajectory; accept the trade-off of less control for stability and focus.
pull_quote: You are no longer in charge of your destiny. Your boss’s opinion actually matters for your career at this new company. In exchange for this loss of control, you get a steady salary and benefits.

episode_title: This Week #15: When should a SaaS startup raise money 💰
insight: Hiring before achieving Product/Market Fit (PMF) often hinders progress, while hiring post-PMF significantly accelerates growth.
pull_quote: “In general, hiring before you get product/market fit slows you down, and hiring after you get product market fit speeds you up. Until you get product/market fit, you want to a) live as long as possible and b) iterate as quickly as possible.”

episode_title: This Week #15: When should a SaaS startup raise money 💰
insight: Spending heavily on growth before solidifying Product/Market Fit is 'premature scaling' and a leading cause of startup failure.
pull_quote: “One of the most common ways that startups die is premature scaling, a term first used by Steve Blank. A business is scaling prematurely if it is spending significant amounts of money on growth before it has discovered and developed PMF.”

episode_title: This Week #15: When should a SaaS startup raise money 💰
insight: Only raise capital when you're confident it will achieve a higher growth rate, and that increased growth justifies the resulting dilution.
pull_quote: “Raise capital when you are confident that you can use it to attain a higher growth rate and the higher growth rate is worth a trade-off in dilution. Higher growth rates increase the value of a business. Higher growth rates fetch greater multiples.”

episode_title: This Week #15: When should a SaaS startup raise money 💰
insight: Successful businesses progress through distinct stages: searching for PMF, then growing, and finally maturing.
pull_quote: Every successful business goes through roughly three stages: 1. Search for product/market fit 2. Growth <— Best to raise money here 3. Maturity

episode_title: This Week #2: Tackling the chicken-and-egg problem, building a growth team from scratch, and addressing overlap with PM peers 🤔
insight: To overcome the marketplace cold start, make your product immediately useful for one side first, using tactics like single-player mode or even subsidizing early supply/demand.
pull_quote: Marketplaces often struggle with the "cold start" problem. The trick is to focus intensely on one side first, like OpenTable handling reservations for restaurants before guest discovery.

episode_title: This Week #2: Tackling the chicken-and-egg problem, building a growth team from scratch, and addressing overlap with PM peers 🤔
insight: Product Managers build new product features, while Program Managers use existing products to drive broader initiatives, leveraging ops, sales, and marketing.
pull_quote: Many companies blur the lines, but a Product Manager's core work is building with engineers. A Program Manager focuses on using existing products with ops, sales, and marketing.

episode_title: This Week #2: Tackling the chicken-and-egg problem, building a growth team from scratch, and addressing overlap with PM peers 🤔
insight: For early-stage growth, pursue 2x-10x top-of-funnel improvements like referrals or performance marketing after healthy retention, not small conversion rate bumps.
pull_quote: Don't chase tiny conversion gains in the early days. Once retention is solid, prioritize 2x-10x growth through top-of-funnel levers like referrals, performance marketing, and SEO.

episode_title: This Week #4: Motivating engineers to hit deadlines, PM career ladders, and aligning with execs
insight: Don't just set deadlines; actively connect engineering work to individual career goals, team mission, and direct incentives like promotions and autonomy.
pull_quote: To motivate engineers, explicitly connect their project work to personal goals like promotions or starting a company. Also, align incentives: shipping quickly should directly impact performance reviews, status, and autonomy.

episode_title: This Week #4: Motivating engineers to hit deadlines, PM career ladders, and aligning with execs
insight: When an engineering team struggles to hit deadlines, prioritize understanding the root causes through genuine inquiry, internal benchmarking, and no-blame post-mortems.
pull_quote: Is your engineering team 'dragging their feet'? Before pushing harder, conduct no-blame post-mortems, ask genuine questions about their process, and benchmark against other teams to uncover the true reasons for delays.

episode_title: This Week #4: Motivating engineers to hit deadlines, PM career ladders, and aligning with execs
insight: When execs give vague 'blue-sky' feedback, define the ideal user experience with no constraints, then work backward to find the most impactful path.
pull_quote: When execs struggle with 'actionable' ideas, don't limit yourself by what's easy. First, envision the 'ideal user experience' with no constraints, then work backward to find what's truly possible and impactful.

episode_title: This Week #5: Overcoming impostor syndrome, introducing growth to an org, and how to partner with your data scientist
insight: Impostor syndrome is a common sign you're in a challenging, successful role, so embrace it as a positive indicator of growth.
pull_quote: Impostor syndrome is normal and is generally a sign that you’re enjoying some degree of success in your life. If you were pumping gas or stacking shelves, you wouldn’t be experiencing impostor syndrome.

episode_title: This Week #5: Overcoming impostor syndrome, introducing growth to an org, and how to partner with your data scientist
insight: To accelerate a growth mindset shift, bring in external experts, benchmark, get CEO buy-in, run small experiments, and become an internal thought leader.
pull_quote: To accelerate a growth team shift: invite experts, benchmark, get CEO buy-in, run a 6-month experiment, and become an internal growth thought leader to influence change.

episode_title: This Week #5: Overcoming impostor syndrome, introducing growth to an org, and how to partner with your data scientist
insight: Treat data scientists as strategic partners by sharing the 'what' and 'why' of your goals, not just queries, to unlock novel insights.
pull_quote: Treat your data scientist as a partner. Share what you are trying to achieve and why, not just the queries you need run. They’ll bring new ideas and novel insights.

episode_title: This Week #5: Overcoming impostor syndrome, introducing growth to an org, and how to partner with your data scientist
insight: Actively prioritize data science requests with your DS, and always articulate what specific action you'll take with the insights to ensure impact.
pull_quote: Data scientists are barraged with questions. Work with them on a public prioritized list, and always have a clear answer for 'What will you do with the answer?'

episode_title: This Week #5: Overcoming impostor syndrome, introducing growth to an org, and how to partner with your data scientist
insight: In highly complex PM roles, maintain perspective, practice mindfulness, ensure shared purpose, and stay in constant dialogue to reduce personal burden.
pull_quote: In highly complex roles, keep your perspective-taking capacity, practice mindfulness, ensure clear shared purpose, and stay in dialogue. You can't do your role alone.

episode_title: This Week #6: Cultivating good relationships with distributed co-workers, building trust to accelerate growth, and leveling up as a PM when you have extra time
insight: Remote work inherently amplifies miscommunication; PMs must foster a culture where relentless clarification and documentation are the norm, not the exception.
pull_quote: Remote work leads to an increase in miscommunication. For engineers and designers, the number one impact is being forced to redo work. Your goal is to create a culture where it’s OK to ask for clarification when something feels unclear.

episode_title: This Week #6: Cultivating good relationships with distributed co-workers, building trust to accelerate growth, and leveling up as a PM when you have extra time
insight: For products reliant on trust, a well-designed referral program is the most powerful growth lever, as friend endorsements bypass the primary adoption barrier.
pull_quote: For a product that relies on trust, there’s nothing more powerful than an endorsement from a friend. Airbnb had a similar trust challenge; referrals proved to be the single biggest attributable growth lever on both sides of the marketplace.

episode_title: This Week #6: Cultivating good relationships with distributed co-workers, building trust to accelerate growth, and leveling up as a PM when you have extra time
insight: In trust-reliant domains like Fintech, immaculate UX polish, down to the smallest detail, is a critical psychological signal for building immediate user trust.
pull_quote: A badly designed site/app immediately introduces suspicion, particularly when dealing with money. Pay attention to the details – image resolution, padding, grammar, performance, and general visual polish. Little things make a big psychological impact here.

episode_title: This Week #6: Cultivating good relationships with distributed co-workers, building trust to accelerate growth, and leveling up as a PM when you have extra time
insight: Accelerate PM growth during time off by pursuing tangential experiential classes or interests, which build leadership, business thinking, or design skills in new contexts.
pull_quote: Product management, as a skillset, is a combination of leadership + business thinking + product design. Instead of reading, take local classes: an adventure sport for leadership, running a business for business thinking, pottery for product design.

episode_title: This Week #7: Effectively communicating about a failure to execs, managing founder expectations, and hiring a Director of PM
insight: When communicating project failure to execs, be upfront, share key learnings, and always present a clear, actionable plan for next steps.
pull_quote: 'This project failed. Let me tell you what happened, what we’ve learned, and what we’re recommending as next steps.' This is how you turn a failure into an opportunity.

episode_title: This Week #7: Effectively communicating about a failure to execs, managing founder expectations, and hiring a Director of PM
insight: To shift a founder's core idea, involve them in testing their own hypotheses, using data to show new directions as an evolution, not failure.
pull_quote: To change a founder’s mind about a core idea, bring them on the journey with you. Align on key hypotheses, test them cheaply, and give the founder a chance to save face by framing it as an evolution.

episode_title: This Week #7: Effectively communicating about a failure to execs, managing founder expectations, and hiring a Director of PM
insight: Hiring a Director of PM? Focus beyond core product skills to assess people leadership, ability to manage large scope, and executive stakeholder influence.
pull_quote: What's different for a Director of PM? They'll be managing PMs, taking on large scope, and working closely with senior leaders. Focus your hiring on these critical differentiating skills.

episode_title: This Week #8: Splitting equity with late-joining co-founders, favorite roadmap templates, and small changes that improve your org
insight: Late-joining co-founders should still get common stock with standard vesting, even if their equity is 10-20%, which is higher than a typical senior hire.
pull_quote: If they’re a true cofounder, would you consider giving them equity starting around 10% of the company — all the way up to a share equal to yours? The stock grant should be common shares with a standard vesting schedule.

episode_title: This Week #8: Splitting equity with late-joining co-founders, favorite roadmap templates, and small changes that improve your org
insight: Top PMs often use simple Google Sheets roadmaps organized by 'lever' (impact area), proving basic tools can outperform complex ones.
pull_quote: I’ve tried a lot of different tools over the years, and I always find myself always coming back to Google Sheets. Option 1: Organized by lever (my preference).

episode_title: This Week #8: Splitting equity with late-joining co-founders, favorite roadmap templates, and small changes that improve your org
insight: Embed monitoring and analytics *with* every feature rollout to proactively gather data that justifies future budget and proves impact.
pull_quote: Delivered monitoring/analytics with each feature implemented in the product. This made it easy to justify more budget by having a backlog of a pretty deep set of analytics.

episode_title: This Week #8: Splitting equity with late-joining co-founders, favorite roadmap templates, and small changes that improve your org
insight: PMs performing a detailed 'Pre-Technical Conception & Legacy study' can accelerate development by 300% by deeply preparing engineers with context.
pull_quote: What I now do as a Product Manager is a ‘Pre-Technical Conception & Legacy study’... Not only did it benefit a lot the team, but it made the project deliveries significantly faster... Our development time of projects went 300% faster.

episode_title: This Week #8: Splitting equity with late-joining co-founders, favorite roadmap templates, and small changes that improve your org
insight: Prioritize addressing 1-2 'UX annoyances' in every sprint alongside bugs to continuously improve product polish and user delight.
pull_quote: For every sprint, we will be picking 1-2 UX Annoyances and adding them to the Backlog. Now we are not just looking at Painkillers (PM vs UX) but also Vitamins!

episode_title: This Week #9: Breaking into growth, leading with influence, and (not) stepping on toes 🦶
insight: To land a growth role, don't just list tactics; demonstrate mastery of foundational tools like data analysis, user psychology, and experimentation.
pull_quote: I see candidates try to impress by listing off a lot of tactical ideas. Your chance of success with this is minimal. Instead, they want to see that you are really strong at the foundational tools.

episode_title: This Week #9: Breaking into growth, leading with influence, and (not) stepping on toes 🦶
insight: When a task stalls in bureaucracy, assume decision-makers don't see its priority; directly ask what it would take to convince them.
pull_quote: By reading between the lines, it’s clear that no one in authority believes what you’re proposing is a priority. Ask them 'What would it take to convince you this is worth prioritizing?'

episode_title: This Week #9: Breaking into growth, leading with influence, and (not) stepping on toes 🦶
insight: If frustrating bureaucratic issues recur, proactively document examples and propose solutions to leaders, shining a mirror on the organizational friction.
pull_quote: If this is a recurring problem, I’d suggest taking initiative and shining a mirror to the org. Put together a short doc with examples and your recommendations for how to fix this problem.

episode_title: This Week #9: Breaking into growth, leading with influence, and (not) stepping on toes 🦶
insight: In ambiguous ownership areas, communicate your initiative, set a clear date for push-back, then proceed to prioritize impact over waiting for explicit approval.
pull_quote: You want to create a culture of impact > ownership. Communicate to leadership you'll tackle a project in an ambiguous area, ask for pushback by a date, and if none, just go for it.

episode_title: This Week #9: Breaking into growth, leading with influence, and (not) stepping on toes 🦶
insight: Organize product teams around 'outcomes' like 'Increase user engagement' rather than specific features to avoid ownership silos and drive true impact.
pull_quote: Airbnb built cross-functional teams around 'Outcomes', not 'Product Features'. This avoids over-optimizing a feature that isn’t driving impact and breaks the idea of clean divisions of ownership.

episode_title: Time management techniques that actually work
insight: Most PM burnout comes from taking on too much, not a lack of productivity tools; say no by filtering asks through priorities and communicating tradeoffs.
pull_quote: Most PM burnout comes from taking on too much, not a lack of productivity tricks. Filter asks through your top priorities and communicate tradeoffs to your manager when saying no – it’s crucial for managing workload.

episode_title: Time management techniques that actually work
insight: Truly valuable product work stems from focused, uninterrupted 'deep work'; fiercely protect recurring blocks and prevent meetings from encroaching.
pull_quote: True value comes from 'deep work'—focused, uninterrupted, cognitively demanding effort. Schedule and fiercely protect recurring deep work blocks; don't let anyone schedule over them.

episode_title: Time management techniques that actually work
insight: Build an 'I got this' aura by proactively tracking all delegated tasks on a 'waiting for' list, freeing mental space and ensuring follow-through.
pull_quote: Highly effective PMs build an 'I got this' aura by never dropping the ball. A 'waiting for' list helps you track every delegated task, freeing your brain and ensuring nothing slips.

episode_title: Time management techniques that actually work
insight: Convert meeting requests to async discussions; many issues resolve faster via email, saving significant time and reducing context-switching overhead.
pull_quote: Before accepting a meeting, try to pivot to async communication. Many meeting requests can be resolved faster via email, saving not just meeting time but also pre- and post-meeting context switching.

episode_title: Top 5 most interesting things about Booking.com's early growth strategy – Issue 46
insight: Deeply integrate performance marketing with product and supply teams, using ad auction losses as signals to identify and fix product or inventory gaps.
pull_quote: When paid marketing is just a function, optimizing campaigns in a cubicle, it doesn’t inform the rest of the business and the funnel doesn’t work. Our supply organization was fully aligned behind what the demand was looking for.

episode_title: Top 5 most interesting things about Booking.com's early growth strategy – Issue 46
insight: Early, high-leverage growth can be driven by a small, specialized, and fiercely competitive team, often outperforming heavy automation and large organizations.
pull_quote: The secret to our success was that we were not heavily automated for most of our early spend. It was actually only two guys: one banker and one coder. Our Google rep was shocked that we had just one person running this.

episode_title: Top 5 most interesting things about Booking.com's early growth strategy – Issue 46
insight: Beyond Product-Market Fit, obsess over Product-Channel Fit by tailoring your product and organization to amplify a dominant acquisition channel's ROI.
pull_quote: People talk about Product-Market Fit. We realized we needed Product-Channel Fit. And it became clear Google AdWords was that for us. Conversion improvements made unviable campaigns viable and improved ROI.

episode_title: Top 5 most interesting things about Booking.com's early growth strategy – Issue 46
insight: Turn competitive disadvantages into strengths by dominating long-tail markets and creating a perception of scale through product and localization.
pull_quote: Unlike our competitors, we didn’t have access to chain hotels in big markets, so we focused on long-tail markets early-on. We could pretend we were a big company in each of these specific cities.

episode_title: Top 5 most interesting things about Booking.com's early growth strategy – Issue 46
insight: Prioritize A/B test results and raw performance metrics, even if they contradict subjective expert opinions or perceived quality, especially in growth.
pull_quote: The localization for our ad copy was done by Google translate, even when we got really big. We asked team members to give us better copy, which we A/B tested, and the Google translated copy often won.

episode_title: Turning around an underperforming team - Issue 17
insight: When taking on a team turnaround, proactively set manager expectations: anticipate lower immediate business impact and potential internal friction for 6-12 months.
pull_quote: It’s critical that your manager, and other influential leaders, understand the situation that you are being put into. It’s likely for the next 6-12 months you’ll drive less business impact than you had previously, and that some team members will become upset with you.

episode_title: Turning around an underperforming team - Issue 17
insight: Don't settle for superficial fixes; define an ambitious vision for what the team *could* become and relentlessly push for exceptional quality.
pull_quote: Don’t let that happen. Set a high bar for yourself and for the team. Work backwards from what you believe this team *could* become, get the team excited about this vision, and don’t stop pushing until you get there.

episode_title: Turning around an underperforming team - Issue 17
insight: To successfully drive team change, connect your proposed solutions directly to individual team members' personal motivations, like impact, promotion, or work-life balance.
pull_quote: Figure out what motivates the key members of the team, and connect the changes you want to make with their personal motivations. Show how issues are slowing down impact, keeping careers back, or causing unnecessary work.

episode_title: Turning around an underperforming team - Issue 17
insight: For effective team turnarounds, rigorously *observe* facts and *identify* root causes first, then *share* recommendations, and finally *act*—avoiding impulsive changes.
pull_quote: Don't rush into fixing an underperforming team. Start by collecting facts, then identify root causes. Only after you’ve spent the time observing, identifying, and communicating, should you finally make the necessary changes.

episode_title: Turning around an underperforming team - Issue 17
insight: Before proposing major changes, identify which team members are allies, detractors, or neutral; this understanding informs your communication and buy-in strategy.
pull_quote: When you determine what needs to change, you’ll find some people are 100% aligned, some completely disagree, and the rest are unsure. Figure out early-on who’s going to back you up, and who you’re going to have to convince.

episode_title: Types of business models
insight: Choose your business model carefully; each has a specific core metric—like GMV for marketplaces or engagement for subscriptions—that you must optimize for success.
pull_quote: Forget vague business plans. There are only eight core ways to make money, and each one demands focus on a specific metric: marketplaces optimize GMV, subscriptions thrive on engagement.

episode_title: Types of business models
insight: To innovate, combine two classic business models, such as selling a product with a recurring subscription, to create novel value and revenue streams.
pull_quote: The secret to innovative business models often lies in combining two existing ones. Think Peloton: they sell a thing (bike) AND charge a subscription (classes), creating a powerful hybrid.

episode_title: Types of business models
insight: Don't just optimize; disrupt by replacing an established business model with a fundamentally different one to unlock new markets or efficiencies.
pull_quote: The biggest disruptions come from replacing, not just tweaking, business models. Uber didn't improve taxis; it replaced the 'rent a thing' model with a 'take rate' platform.

episode_title: Using your working time well - Issue 22
insight: Most PMs default to shallow work; intentionally blocking and fiercely defending deep work time is crucial to create value and grow skills.
pull_quote: As a PM, it's remarkably easy for *all of your time* to be spent doing shallow work—answering emails, moving JIRA tasks, endless meetings. But only distraction-free deep work creates new value and improves your skills. Block off specific time and defend it fiercely to avoid having your efforts be easily replicated.

episode_title: Using your working time well - Issue 22
insight: Stop wasting time re-figuring out what's next; establish a simple, trusted TODO system with concrete action priorities and a list of blockers.
pull_quote: A lot of PM time is wasted re-figuring out what to work on, or convincing your brain to tackle vague problems. The solution is a simple, trusted TODO system: concrete action priorities, not vague projects, and a clear list of people blocking your progress.

episode_title: Using your working time well - Issue 22
insight: PMs easily get lost in shallow tasks; aggressively scheduling and protecting deep work blocks is vital for creating value and developing skills.
pull_quote: As a PM, it's remarkably easy for *all of your time* to be spent doing shallow work — answering emails, moving JIRA tasks, going to endless meetings. Finding time for deep work is essential.

episode_title: Using your working time well - Issue 22
insight: Avoid wasting time deciding what's next by using a simple, trusted TODO system that clearly lists concrete actions and externalizes all mental load.
pull_quote: A lot of time is wasted re-figuring out what to work on. Getting your to-dos out of your head and into a system you can refer to clears your mind of any mental distractions that will keep you from working efficiently.

episode_title: Using your working time well - Issue 22
insight: When new tasks arrive, don't just say yes or no; prioritize them against your current workload and transparently communicate the new sequencing to your manager.
pull_quote: When you’ve already got a full plate and your manager comes to you with more work, the two wrong answers are: Yes! and No! Instead, prioritize it and communicate where this ask now sits.

episode_title: Using your working time well - Issue 22
insight: Staying ahead simplifies everything; proactively anticipate team blockers and manager asks, then start addressing them before they become urgent.
pull_quote: The more ahead of everyone else you are, the easier everything gets. Always be asking yourself — What are the next biggest blockers for my team? What is my manager likely to ask of me?

episode_title: Virality is a myth (mostly)
insight: Most "viral" product growth isn't user-to-user exponential spread but rather a few influential sources broadcasting to many people at once.
pull_quote: Much of what outsiders call virality is really a function of what one might call ‘dark broadcasters’—people or companies distributing information to many viewers at once, but whose influence isn’t always visible.

episode_title: Virality is a myth (mostly)
insight: True product virality (k-factor > 1) is exceptionally rare, short-lived, and quickly exhausts user networks, leading to linear growth.
pull_quote: The problem with a word-of-mouth business and exponential growth is people run out of people to talk to. That’s the limiting factor. Networks get exhausted.

episode_title: Virality is a myth (mostly)
insight: Sustained product growth requires both built-in virality mechanisms and repeated, intentional "one-to-many" broadcast events like PR or influencer campaigns.
pull_quote: The higher your k-factor, the more juice you’ll get from each broadcast. But you’ll get no juice if you don’t find a way to get your product in front of a huge number of people at once.

episode_title: Virality is a myth (mostly)
insight: Zooming out reveals that even "viral" products like Facebook and Instagram exhibit mostly linear growth over the long term, not continuous exponential.
pull_quote: Looking at these charts, it surprised me how non-viral the growth of most “viral” products looks when you zoom out. Viral moments are short-lived accelerations, sparked by one-off broadcast events.

episode_title: What is a good activation rate
insight: A truly effective activation milestone is both highly predictive of long-term retention (at least 2x) and directly actionable by growth teams.
pull_quote: A good activation metric must be two things: highly predictive of long-term value delivery, showing 2x better retention for activated users. It also needs to be highly actionable, something growth teams can directly impact.

episode_title: What is a good activation rate
insight: Average activation is 34%, but rates vary wildly (10-70%) by product type and the effort required for your defined milestone.
pull_quote: The average activation rate is 34%, but numbers vary wildly by product type. Marketplaces often see 10-20% due to a first transaction milestone, while B2C freemium products can hit 50-70% with low-friction actions.

episode_title: What is a good activation rate
insight: Don't define activation too early (just signup) or too late (multiple purchases); ensure your milestone is predictive and actionable, not complicated.
pull_quote: The most common mistake when setting your activation milestone is defining it too early or too late. Activation is a leading indicator of habit formation, not merely signup or already a long-term customer.

episode_title: What is a good activation rate
insight: Initially, assume activation correlation; prove causality by experimenting to improve the metric and monitoring the uplift in later-stage retention.
pull_quote: Your activation event is initially just a hypothesis and correlative. Prove causality by running experiments to improve it, then monitor if later-stage metrics uptick. Don't overthink it; pick a strongly correlated milestone and go.

episode_title: What is a good growth rate
insight: For early B2B, investors largely ignore MoM growth; instead, they obsess over how fast you hit $1M ARR post-launch, often preferring no data to weak data.
pull_quote: In the early stages, investors don’t care about month-over-month growth. Percentages with a small base don’t mean much. For B2B, getting to $1M ARR in 12 months or shorter is great. No data is often better than bad/small data.

episode_title: What is a good growth rate
insight: Don't chase early growth before solid retention; investors value strong customer love, high efficiency, and quality ARR over raw, unsustainable growth numbers.
pull_quote: It’s a huge mistake to focus on growth before getting retention. You can hit 'great' early numbers by brute force, but that won’t translate. Investors prioritize robust retention, efficiency, and quality of ARR over raw, unsustainable growth.

episode_title: What is a good growth rate
insight: For consumer products, forget early revenue; investors deeply care about intense engagement, strong retention (e.g., >50% DAU/MAU or D30), and organic virality.
pull_quote: For early-stage consumer businesses, it’s all about intensity of engagement, virality, and retention—not revenue. >50% DAU/MAU or >50% D30 retention is excellent. If you have both, only then do investors worry about growth and monetization.

episode_title: What is a good growth rate
insight: Growth benchmarks are merely rules of thumb; in hot categories, investors often disregard them, recognizing that many paths—even slow starts—can lead to massive success.
pull_quote: All growth benchmarks are just rules of thumb. Investors consider growth rates one piece of the puzzle, alongside founder/market fit and retention. In a hot category, these fundamentals often go right out the window.

episode_title: What is a good payback period?
insight: When calculating payback period, always use gross profit, not revenue, as revenue doesn't cover salaries, only gross profit does.
pull_quote: If a startup acquires a customer for $100, and that customer generates $10/mo in revenue, with 80% gross margins ($8 gross profit), the payback period is 12.5 months. Not 10 months.

episode_title: What is a good payback period?
insight: A lower payback period isn't always optimal; aggressively extending it can unlock more profitable growth for mature businesses with predictable LTV.
pull_quote: A 10-year-old consumer business with a one-month payback is likely leaving profitable growth on the table. You should grow more aggressively by strategically extending your payback period.

episode_title: What is a good payback period?
insight: For businesses with significant paid growth, always track payback by channel, not blended, to reveal true efficiency and customer quality.
pull_quote: If paid marketing drives most of your growth, your blended payback period is often a 'useless metric' because it will mask the true efficiency of individual channels.

episode_title: What is a good payback period?
insight: Your ideal payback period depends heavily on your business model's loyalty, frequency, and purchase cost, not just industry benchmarks.
pull_quote: For low-loyalty, high-frequency products like mobile games, a 1-3 month payback is needed. But for high-cost, sticky services like phone providers, 1-2 years can be worth it.

episode_title: What is good free-to-paid conversion
insight: Free trial products convert 2-3x better than freemium (15-25% vs. 6-8% great performance), largely due to higher user intent and more sales involvement.
pull_quote: For a freemium self-serve product, 3%-5% is GOOD, 6%-8% is GREAT. Free trial products have higher conversion rates—GOOD is 8%-12%, and 15%-25% is GREAT.

episode_title: What is good free-to-paid conversion
insight: Expect significantly lower free-to-paid conversion when targeting larger companies or developer audiences versus prosumers or general SMBs.
pull_quote: If you’re selling to larger customers, plan for lower free-to-paid conversion. The median conversion rate for developer-focused companies was 5%; half that of companies not selling to developers.

episode_title: What is good free-to-paid conversion
insight: Layering sales outreach onto free users (product-led sales) significantly boosts free-to-paid conversion, making combined PLG and sales more efficient.
pull_quote: A main driver of increasing free-to-paid conversion is simple: having sales reach out to free accounts. Nearly half of free-trial companies have sales reach out to more than half of sign-ups.

episode_title: What is good free-to-paid conversion
insight: Prioritize "activated sign-ups" over total sign-ups as a KPI; making signup too easy can attract low-intent users who inflate numbers but never convert.
pull_quote: Companies celebrate an acceleration in sign-ups only to realize the extra sign-ups never translated into more customers. Replace sign-ups as a core marketing KPI with activated sign-ups.

episode_title: What is good free-to-paid conversion
insight: Reverse trials, offering a premium trial then downgrading to free, double conversion rates of classic freemium while maintaining similar sign-up rates.
pull_quote: Reverse trials are a relatively new phenomenon—only 5% of respondents had one. Early data shows they convert at twice the rate of classic freemium while maintaining a similar sign-up rate.

episode_title: What is good monthly churn
insight: While monthly churn is quick and easy, *cohort retention* and *net revenue retention* are more critical long-term predictors of product health and growth potential.
pull_quote: Monthly churn is powerful for short-term checks, but it blends new and existing users. For long-term health, focus on cohort retention and net revenue retention to understand what's really going on.

episode_title: What is good monthly churn
insight: Good monthly churn varies drastically by business type and price point; B2B Enterprise charging $5K+ needs less than 0.35% monthly churn.
pull_quote: Don't compare your churn to others blindly. A healthy monthly churn rate is highly dependent on your price point. The higher the price, the lower you should expect it to be—under 0.35% for B2B at $5K+.

episode_title: What is good monthly churn
insight: High churn in the first 1-3 months often signals an activation/onboarding failure or acquiring the wrong customers, not necessarily a core product issue.
pull_quote: Month 1-3 churn is the steepest, often 5-50%. It usually indicates a failure in activation/onboarding, or you're acquiring the wrong kind of customer, especially with paid marketing.

episode_title: What is good monthly churn
insight: Higher churn rates can be acceptable for startups with low customer acquisition costs, low marginal costs, or if not aiming for venture scale.
pull_quote: Finding your churn higher than benchmarks? Don't despair. It's OK if you have low CAC and marginal costs, or if you're not building a venture-scale business that requires hyper-growth.

episode_title: What is good retention?
insight: Gauge your product's health against concrete retention benchmarks: ~40% 6-month user retention is GOOD for Consumer SaaS, ~100% 12-month NRR for Bottom-up SaaS.
pull_quote: What's 'good' retention? For Consumer SaaS, it's ~40% 6-month user retention; for Bottom-up SaaS, ~100% 12-month net revenue retention. These benchmarks, informed by 20 growth experts, provide concrete targets.

episode_title: What is good retention?
insight: Retention is the single most important metric for sustainable growth and the truest indicator of product-market fit, driving all acquisition strategies.
pull_quote: Retention is *the* scalable way to grow a product, the best indicator of product-market fit, and the most important factor in user lifetime value. Yet, it's one of the least understood metrics.

episode_title: What is good retention?
insight: Lower retention rates can be acceptable if your CAC is very low, you're an early-stage startup, or not aiming for venture-scale growth.
pull_quote: To some, these retention benchmarks will seem high. But lower retention can be OK if you have low CAC, are just starting out, or aren't building a venture-scale business. Retention doesn't live in a vacuum.

episode_title: What is good retention?
insight: Net Revenue Retention benchmarks often exceed 100% for land-and-expand SaaS models, unlike user retention, due to upgrades and expansion revenue.
pull_quote: Don't confuse user and revenue retention. Bottom-up SaaS and 'land and expand' models can achieve >100% net revenue retention (e.g., Twilio 140-170%) by expanding accounts, making it a distinct health metric.

episode_title: What is good waitlist conversion
insight: Waitlist conversion rates plummet over time; expect a 50% average within a month, but under 20% if you wait beyond three.
pull_quote: Waitlist conversion rates drop significantly with time. If you get people off the waitlist in under a month, expect around 50% conversion. Wait over three months, and it falls below 20% for free sign-ups.

episode_title: What is good waitlist conversion
insight: Targeting 'diehard fans' can yield near 90% waitlist conversion, while cheaper products consistently convert better than expensive ones.
pull_quote: Audience quality and product cost are huge conversion drivers. Diehard fans can convert at almost 90%, but free products always convert better than paid software, which beats physical goods.

episode_title: What is good waitlist conversion
insight: For older waitlist cohorts, treat it as a full re-education funnel; actively warm the list with content and re-introduce the product value.
pull_quote: Don't just launch; actively 'warm' your waitlist with content. For older segments, assume they've forgotten your product and take a full-funnel approach to re-educate them.

episode_title: What is good waitlist conversion
insight: Validate waitlist emails to prevent spam flags and send multiple re-engagement emails to remind users of your product's value.
pull_quote: Bounces can land you in spam, so validate waitlist emails with a service like ZeroBounce. Also, don't send just one invite; re-engage users with at least three emails.

episode_title: What is product management
insight: A PM's core job is delivering business impact by marshaling team resources to identify and solve the most impactful customer problems.
pull_quote: Your job as a PM is to deliver business impact by marshaling the resources of your team to identify and solve the most impactful customer problems.

episode_title: What is product management
insight: A PM's true output isn't their own work, but amplifying the entire cross-functional team's output to deliver business impact.
pull_quote: Your job isn’t to do the building yourself but instead to increase the leverage of your cross-functional teammates... a product manager’s output = the output of their team.

episode_title: What is product management
insight: PMs have three core jobs: shaping the product with insights, shipping high-quality products on time, and crucially, synchronizing all stakeholders.
pull_quote: Another way to define the role of a PM is to break out the day-to-day jobs: Shape the product, Ship the product, and Synchronize the people.

episode_title: What is product management
insight: The PM role is never typical; it varies dramatically across companies and between startups vs. big companies in required skills and influence.
pull_quote: The PM role is rarely typical. If you’re looking to get into PM, it’s important that you look into how the company you work at specifically looks at the PM role. There are two axes across which the PM role most varies: Skills and Influence.

episode_title: What is product management
insight: If you're starting as a PM, focus on mastering communication, collaboration, execution, customer insights, and strategic thinking to thrive.
pull_quote: If you’re just starting out [as a PM], I’d suggest focusing on these five: Communication, Collaboration, Execution, Customer insights, Strategy. You’ll need to develop each of these to thrive long-term.

episode_title: What it feels like when you've found product-market fit
insight: Many successful products don't hit PMF immediately; it often takes months or years of iteration, becoming clear through compounding wins.
pull_quote: Don't expect PMF to be a 'big bang' at launch. My research shows half of iconic companies iterated for months or years to find it, becoming clear through compounding wins.

episode_title: What it feels like when you've found product-market fit
insight: Undeniable market pull for PMF includes customers asking to pay, complaining when features are missing, or even using a broken product.
pull_quote: Real market pull isn't just traffic. It's users asking 'Why won't you take my money?', panicking when your site goes down, or using your product even when it's broken.

episode_title: What it feels like when you've found product-market fit
insight: The intensity of market pull is a function of both your product's 'fit' for a problem and the size of your initial target market.
pull_quote: The PMF pull isn't uniform. A 10x product in a huge market creates sudden, broad pull (Dropbox). A great product in a niche market sees steady, compounding pull (Instacart).

episode_title: What it feels like when you've found product-market fit
insight: Beyond building what people want, true PMF demands profitability at scale and a sustainable way to acquire and retain customers.
pull_quote: Everyone focuses on 'building what people want' for PMF. But true, *sustainable* PMF requires profit at scale and finding/keeping customers sustainably. Missing any kills you.

episode_title: What jury duty taught me about product management
insight: A third of a trial is spent selecting the jury; PMs should apply similar rigor to team and meeting attendee selection to avoid derailment.
pull_quote: A trial lasts three days, and one whole day is for jury selection. The prosecution and defense spend as much time picking *who* they're pitching as doing the actual pitching. PMs should apply this same rigor.

episode_title: What jury duty taught me about product management
insight: Previewing your entire argument in opening statements prevents your audience from jumping to premature conclusions and builds alignment.
pull_quote: What surprised me most was how impactful opening arguments were. Each side told us exactly what their case would be, preventing premature conclusions. This framework works for execs too.

episode_title: What jury duty taught me about product management
insight: Treat important decision meetings like lawyers treat trials: do the pre-work through one-on-one discussions to ensure low-drama and no surprises.
pull_quote: Trials are low-drama because lawyers never ask a question they don't already know the answer to. Do the pre-work with stakeholders to ensure your important PM meetings are low-drama and full of non-surprises.

episode_title: What jury duty taught me about product management
insight: Misalignment is inevitable; the highest-leverage work for PMs is proactively hunting for it and using source-of-truth docs to re-align everyone quickly.
pull_quote: When a witness created doubt, the prosecutor brought the jury back into alignment. Highly effective PMs endlessly hunt for misalignment and quickly push everyone back into alignment. This is highest-leverage work.

episode_title: What jury duty taught me about product management
insight: The defense attorney wasn't great but won. Influence boils down to compelling evidence and the degree to which people trust you and your information.
pull_quote: The judge instructed us to base our decision solely on evidence and trusted accounts, ignoring attorney speculation or emotion. The defense attorney was not great but won. Influence truly boils down to evidence + trust.

episode_title: What people are vibe coding (and actually using)
insight: AI tools empower product builders to solve 'n-of-1' hyper-specific problems, making previously uneconomical niche markets viable.
pull_quote: Welcome to the era of n-of-1 personalized software. Everyone is solving their own hyper-specific problem, often leading to products used by tens/hundreds/thousands of others.

episode_title: What people are vibe coding (and actually using)
insight: Solving your own 'stupidly specific' problem with AI tools is an accelerated path to uncovering broader user needs and unexpected product adoption.
pull_quote: Even though you’re solving your own problem, many of the products end up being used by tens/hundreds/thousands of other people. This is an excellent sign, revealing unexpected market demand.

episode_title: What people are vibe coding (and actually using)
insight: PMs can now 'vibe code' functional prototypes by simply describing requirements in plain English to AI tools, then iterating conversationally.
pull_quote: Simply describe what you want in plain English, as if you were talking to a remote engineer. Then, iterate by describing what you want to change. You’ll be surprised by how far you’ll get.

episode_title: What Seven Years at Airbnb Taught Me About Building a Business
insight: Vague problem statements drag projects for months, while crisp, aligned problem statements make complex projects sail smoothly.
pull_quote: Crafting and aligning on a problem statement is the single most important step in solving any problem. I’ve consistently seen simple projects with vague problem statements go in circles for weeks and months, while complex projects with strong problem statements sail smoothly.

episode_title: What Seven Years at Airbnb Taught Me About Building a Business
insight: Set "uncomfortable" goals—even 2x or 10x your proposed target—to compel teams to think bigger and achieve the impossible.
pull_quote: Airbnb’s CEO, Brian Chesky, is infamous for doubling our proposed goals, and often pushing us to 10 times the goal. This ambitious approach pushed teams to think bigger and rise to the occasion, often getting incredibly close to the target.

episode_title: What Seven Years at Airbnb Taught Me About Building a Business
insight: Instead of incremental optimization, envision the perfect user experience, like Disney's Snow White storyboards, then work backward for step-function change.
pull_quote: A variation of Amazon’s working backward methodology starts with envisioning the perfect user experience. A classic example was 'Snow White,' where the team developed storyboards of the ideal guest and host experience, identifying key emotional moments along that journey.

episode_title: What Seven Years at Airbnb Taught Me About Building a Business
insight: As a leader, your most important "product" is how you organize people; optimize for autonomous, cross-functional teams with clear mandates.
pull_quote: As you move up the ranks of leadership, you quickly learn the most important product to get right is how you organize your people. Optimize for dedicated cross-functional teams with a clear mandate, so they can move autonomously toward an agreed-upon goal.

episode_title: What Seven Years at Airbnb Taught Me About Building a Business
insight: Extreme focus drives significant gains: reduce problem space for teams and eliminate distractions for users to improve impact and conversion.
pull_quote: I found teams unable to build real momentum until I reduced their problem space and provided a focused mandate. Similarly, some of Airbnb’s biggest guest conversion gains came from simple tweaks that gave users fewer things to think about.

episode_title: What They'd Do Differently 🔮 Kickstarting and Scaling a Marketplace Business
insight: Scaling without proven Product-Market Fit (PMF) on core metrics like fill rate leads to churn and wasted growth, as TaskRabbit learned.
pull_quote: At TaskRabbit, we scaled before we had product market fit. We should have focused on fill rate; people who didn’t get matched never came back. The company instead skewed too far into top-line growth.

episode_title: What They'd Do Differently 🔮 Kickstarting and Scaling a Marketplace Business
insight: Product teams often misallocate resources, with hundreds working on low-impact areas while critical growth drivers receive minimal attention, as seen at Etsy.
pull_quote: At Etsy, hundreds of people worked on pages selling 700 items daily, while only three or four handled search, which sold 100,000 items. Our product team's activity often had nothing to do with actual growth.

episode_title: What They'd Do Differently 🔮 Kickstarting and Scaling a Marketplace Business
insight: Short-term product decisions, like OpenTable's exclusivity, can create long-term strategic debt, hindering growth by limiting platform breadth.
pull_quote: At OpenTable, a critical early decision helped short-term but bit us later: requiring full use to be listed. This allowed competitors like Yelp to become better known by listing everyone, disappointing users.

episode_title: What They'd Do Differently 🔮 Kickstarting and Scaling a Marketplace Business
insight: Neglecting empathy for your supply-side users (e.g., drivers) leads to tactical, short-sighted product decisions that damage long-term marketplace health.
pull_quote: It took us a long time at Lyft to build customer empathy for drivers. We were too direct, too forceful, making tactical, short-sighted changes instead of understanding the holistic impact on driver experience.

episode_title: What They'd Do Differently 🔮 Kickstarting and Scaling a Marketplace Business
insight: Even with high traffic, many scaled products stop continuous, low-effort A/B testing too early, missing easy wins and valuable insights.
pull_quote: At Lyft, we lost our scrappy, iterative mindset too early, wasting traffic by not running more tests. We used to launch 1-2 tests a week with low effort, learning critical insights for messaging or driver behavior.

episode_title: What to ask your users about Product-Market Fit
insight: To measure Product-Market Fit, ask users how disappointed they'd be without your product, how it compares to alternatives, and what they *actually* used last.
pull_quote: Sean Ellis suggests 40% of users saying 'very disappointed' if your product was gone is the PMF threshold. Complement this affection metric with questions on utility and behavioral usage to triangulate fit.

episode_title: What to ask your users about Product-Market Fit
insight: When interviewing users for PMF, first understand their core task and alternatives before digging into their specific experience, hopes, and satisfaction with your product.
pull_quote: In PMF interviews, listen for 'explicit, superior value relative to competitors' and 'genuine enthusiasm.' If users only *want* to love your product for its potential, you haven't hit PMF yet.

episode_title: What to ask your users about Product-Market Fit
insight: Avoid asking NPS or directly querying what users 'want' when seeking PMF signals, as these questions are often unreliable and provide misleading data.
pull_quote: Don't fall for the 'NPS Industrial Complex' or blindly ask users what they want. These common research questions are often poor indicators of PMF, as users don't always know what they truly need.

episode_title: What to ask your users about Product-Market Fit
insight: Achieve stronger Product-Market Fit by triangulating insights from diverse user feedback, and deliberately attempting to falsify your product's core assumptions.
pull_quote: No single magic question reveals PMF. Instead, triangulate insights from multiple research approaches. Focus on trying to *falsify* your product's underlying assumptions to discover unexpected paths to fit.

episode_title: What to do if your product isn’t taking off
insight: Don't confuse people rooting for you with market signal; true product validation requires observing specific "pain" and "pull" from users, like paying or strong emotion.
pull_quote: If I drill down what makes companies fail, it’s quite simple: They don’t talk to users, which means they don’t find product-market fit. If they don’t find product-market fit, nothing else really matters.

episode_title: What to do if your product isn’t taking off
insight: To accelerate growth, define your target audience "almost comically narrow" with at least three characteristics, as this hyper-focus drives adoption and social proof.
pull_quote: Your target audience should also be very narrow. Almost comically narrow. Shoot for at least three narrowing characteristics. Pinterest initially targeted "30-something female bloggers."

episode_title: What to do if your product isn’t taking off
insight: Achieving "message-market fit" with a clear, differentiated pitch can attract interest and drive adoption even before your product fully delivers on its promise.
pull_quote: When we launched, I think we had ‘message-market fit.’ We were able to differentiate and have people be interested in our unique messaging, but we didn’t have the product there yet.

episode_title: What to do if your product isn’t taking off
insight: When a product struggles, identify any single feature or piece of functionality that *is* showing strong pull and pivot your entire focus to that specific success.
pull_quote: Something like 20% of successful consumer companies pivoted at least once... and a whopping 40% in B2B. Instagram pivoted from a check-in app by doubling down on photo sharing.

episode_title: What to do if your product isn’t taking off
insight: Strategic quitting is often a "genius" move, not failure, for founders and PMs, freeing up resources and focus for higher-conviction endeavors.
pull_quote: Winners quit fast, quit often, and quit without guilt. Sometimes knowing when to give up, when to try something else, is genius. Giving up doesn’t mean stopping. Don’t ever stop.

episode_title: What working at Figma taught me about customer obsession
insight: Engineers spending 20% of their time on customer support might seem inefficient, but it actually accelerates product velocity by quickly revealing what truly matters to users.
pull_quote: Figma's engineers spent 20% of their time on customer support, initially seeming to slow development. But this direct contact built a reputation for responsiveness and helped the team learn faster what customers needed.

episode_title: What working at Figma taught me about customer obsession
insight: Shift from a product-oriented mindset to a service-oriented one: your software is merely a tool to help people achieve their higher-level goals, not the goal itself.
pull_quote: Forget building 'better products.' Figma inverts 'jobs to be done' by viewing software as a *service*. The product is just a tool; the ultimate goal is helping people do great design.

episode_title: What working at Figma taught me about customer obsession
insight: For high-frequency products, prioritize 'feel' (performance, familiarity, consistency) over a broad feature set; a polished core builds credibility faster than a feature-rich but clunky product.
pull_quote: When Figma launched, they cut multiplayer and other key features. Why? Starting with something that felt bad—even with all the right features—would have created a lasting credibility problem. Polish built trust for future features.

episode_title: What's in your software stack — Product, Design, Analytics, DS, Research, and more
insight: Communication tools like Slack are more central to a PM's daily stack than dedicated project management software.
pull_quote: Slack was the most mentioned tool in the product management stack, ahead of Jira, Productboard, and Asana. It confirms PMs spend more time coordinating and sharing knowledge than solely managing tasks.

episode_title: What's in your software stack — Product, Design, Analytics, DS, Research, and more
insight: Early-stage startups first invest in foundational communication, collaboration, and no-code automation, not niche specialized tools.
pull_quote: Slack, G-Suite, GitHub, and Notion are the top tools early startups buy immediately. No-code solutions like Zapier also rank high, proving their immediate value for operational efficiency.

episode_title: What's in your software stack — Product, Design, Analytics, DS, Research, and more
insight: Customer communication platforms and core cloud infrastructure rapidly become a startup's largest software expenditures.
pull_quote: Intercom was the most expensive tool mentioned by startups, ahead of AWS and G-Suite. This highlights the high cost and perceived value of strong customer engagement and scalable infrastructure.

episode_title: What's in your software stack — Product, Design, Analytics, DS, Research, and more
insight: General-purpose collaboration tools like Notion and Miro are now essential across product, design, and user research functions.
pull_quote: Notion and Miro aren't just for PMs; they appear prominently in design and user research stacks too. These flexible tools are becoming the universal backbone for team collaboration and knowledge sharing.

episode_title: What’s in your stack: The state of tech tools in 2025
insight: AI tools like ChatGPT are now more essential to daily work than email or internal chat, with over 90% of tech professionals using them regularly.
pull_quote: AI tools have become as essential as having a laptop. A whopping 90% of respondents use ChatGPT regularly. More participants use ChatGPT than Gmail (76%) or Slack (71%) 🤯

episode_title: What’s in your stack: The state of tech tools in 2025
insight: Bundling offers lock-in, but superior user experience drives disruption: hated incumbents like Jira lose to well-crafted alternatives like Linear.
pull_quote: Jira dominates project management but tops the 'please let us switch' list. Bundling is powerful, but it can get you only so far: users are willing to ditch 'least valued' tools for better-crafted alternatives like Linear.

episode_title: What’s in your stack: The state of tech tools in 2025
insight: Tools 'good enough at everything' like Notion, with broad flexibility, are disrupting specialized incumbents across multiple product categories.
pull_quote: Notion is playing a clever game: it's the second-most-popular project management tool, fourth-most-popular docs, and gaining traction as a CRM. Participants praise its flexibility: 'It’s my go-to for … anything!'

episode_title: What’s in your stack: The state of tech tools in 2025
insight: Savvy teams mix and match multiple competing tools within the same category, curating a specialized stack for nuanced context and specific needs.
pull_quote: People are mixing and matching different tools in the same space. For core jobs, people use several (competing) tools within the same category, choosing the perfect one for each nuanced context or need.

episode_title: When and how to run a billboard campaign
insight: Don't expect clean, direct-response ROI metrics for brand awareness campaigns; instead, measure a spectrum of indirect indicators like branded search, pipeline mentions, and internal morale.
pull_quote: Unlike with digital demand generation, attribution for physical ads is murky and imperfect. You may never get a big final number to share in your board presentation, and that’s OK.

episode_title: When and how to run a billboard campaign
insight: To make brand campaigns memorable, either invest in high frequency or craft a simple, emotionally resonant, and potentially controversial message that sparks discussion.
pull_quote: To go viral, something has to be *new* and *slightly controversial*. A simple, compelling creative concept that elicits an emotional response is key for virality and recall.

episode_title: When and how to run a billboard campaign
insight: Physical brand campaigns aren't standalone; amplify their impact by integrating them with digital channels, community events, and social media for a holistic 'experience'.
pull_quote: We don’t just think of OOH as ads; we think of them as experiences. The more you integrate your OOH strategy with complementary channels, frequency and resonance go up—and the more effective each can be.

episode_title: When and how to run a billboard campaign
insight: Maximize physical ad impact by precisely targeting locations based on your ICP's daily movements and aligning campaigns with major industry events or product launches.
pull_quote: For out-of-home, it’s not enough to know who your ideal buyer is; you have to understand their physical habits—where they live, where they work, and how they move back and forth.

episode_title: When and how to run a billboard campaign
insight: Always allocate budget for paid search ads on your company name during brand campaigns; failing to protect branded search allows competitors to capture interested users.
pull_quote: One tactical tip: consider setting aside funds to 'protect your brand' by buying paid search ads related to your company name. You want to make sure they find *you* and not a wall of ads from competitors.

episode_title: When NOT to run an experiment – Issue 54
insight: Don't run A/B tests until you have enough traffic; a 5% conversion lift on a 10% step often needs 60,000+ users per variation.
pull_quote: To detect even a 5% conversion increase on a 10% step, you need over 60,000 unique users per variation. For many startups, waiting that long for results is simply not worth it.

episode_title: When NOT to run an experiment – Issue 54
insight: Skip experiments for low-risk, high-effort changes; your team's time is better spent improving the experimentation process itself.
pull_quote: If running an experiment is a painful, time-consuming process for a low-risk change, just ship it. You might gain more by investing that time into making experiments easier to run.

episode_title: When NOT to run an experiment – Issue 54
insight: Don't A/B test a completely new product or major pivot; you lack a control group, so set independent success metrics instead.
pull_quote: Launching something completely new or pivoting your product means no control to compare against. Don't force an A/B test; instead, define clear independent success criteria.

episode_title: When they hired their first PM
insight: Over half of companies, especially B2B, hire their first PM *before* finding product-market fit, defying common wisdom.
pull_quote: Surprisingly, more than half of companies hired their first PM *before* finding product-market fit, which is counter to the advice you hear. Particularly in B2B.

episode_title: When they hired their first PM
insight: The first PM's primary role is execution and bringing order, *not* defining product strategy, which remains the founder's job.
pull_quote: Do not delegate your strategy to your first product manager. It’s the founders’ jobs to set the strategic direction. The main benefit is diligent execution support and preventing small but important things from falling through the cracks.

episode_title: When they hired their first PM
insight: Founders must proactively *give* trust to their first PM, enabling delegation and coaching through mistakes, rather than expecting trust to be earned.
pull_quote: The biggest problem I see is when founders don’t really want to cede control. Ask yourself if you’re ready to trust someone else. Choose to trust them on principle, not wait for it to be earned.

episode_title: When they hired their first PM
insight: Many successful companies hire their first PM from internal transfers (engineers, analysts), leveraging their deep product and customer knowledge.
pull_quote: Many first PMs come from internal transfers. Robinhood's first PM was an iOS engineer. Segment's first PM was a forward-deployed engineer who started prioritizing fixes and working with customers.

episode_title: When they hired their first PM
insight: Early PMs are most valuable in complex B2B products; in straightforward B2C, they can disempower engineering and design without clear purpose.
pull_quote: Early PMs are mostly useful in B2B and can be a hindrance in B2C. If the customer relationship is complex, hire a PM sooner. In simpler consumer experiences, a PM can disempower engineers and designers.

episode_title: When to hire your first product manager
insight: Whether you hire a PM or not, someone *must* perform the critical jobs of shaping, shipping, and synchronizing the product and people.
pull_quote: Whether you hire a full-time PM or not, someone on a product team will need to do the jobs of a PM. These jobs are shaping the product, shipping it, and synchronizing the people.

episode_title: When to hire your first product manager
insight: Hire your first PM when faced with frequent bottlenecks, constant team misalignment, or a burning need to deeply tackle a problem post-product-market fit.
pull_quote: It's time to hire your first product manager when you see frequent bottlenecks blocking progress, constant team misalignment on priorities, or a burning need to go deep on a key problem after achieving Product/Market Fit.

episode_title: When to hire your first product manager
insight: Hiring your first PM too early, especially pre-product-market fit, often creates "too many cooks" and unnecessary overhead, hindering crucial early agility.
pull_quote: Don't hire your first PM too early. "Many founders I’ve worked with are surprised when my recommendation is often *not yet*," says Ken Norton. "The danger of having too many cooks in the kitchen exceeds the cost of being overworked."

episode_title: When to hire your first product manager
insight: Data shows successful companies typically hire their first PM as employee #15-100, 2-4 years post-founding, often after establishing core product vision.
pull_quote: When do companies hire their first PM? Based on successful startups, it's generally as the 15th to 100th employee, and often 2 to 4 years after founding. This usually happens once core product vision is clearer.

episode_title: When to sunset a feature
insight: Teams almost always wait too long to sunset features and rarely regret killing a product, even those launched with much fanfare.
pull_quote: Not once do I recall that anyone regretted a product or feature being sunset. If anything, teams waited too long to kill these products. Even features launched with much fanfare, like Airbnb's Guidebooks or Wish Lists, were eventually sunset.

episode_title: When to sunset a feature
insight: Use a 5-point checklist (usage, cost, UX, strategy, user outcry) to decide if a feature should be sunset.
pull_quote: If a feature checks three of these five boxes, it's likely time to kill it: Low usage (<5%), High maintenance cost (>10% team resources), Degrades user experience, Misalignment with strategy, or Very few vocal users.

episode_title: When to sunset a feature
